It's Doom, if you don't know this one by now, I can't help you! But everything, everywhere, all at once! The grand daddy of FPS gaming!
My one and only criticism, and it's more recommendation than criticism, is it would be phenominal to have the option to run with the Playstation lighting and SFX enhancement (which I think was the same audio profile as Doom 64, but with the classic skins), because the Playstation Doom really upped the ambience.
I Enjoyed both of these games, Playing through what may be 2 of the most influential video games of all time makes me grateful for almost everything that was made after it

This game had me hooked the first time I played it, I was very late to the game both in year and my age, but I still play it to this day and its one of my favorites. I prefer games with pixely graphics, they're more fun and less hard on the eyes, and they're easier to run on my piece of ♥♥♥♥ laptop, its really a win.

Recommend this game to everyone

A really old game that I'm experiencing for the first time in my life.
It's overall very fun, I'm playing in the hurt me plenty difficulty and until now I only died once (skill issue).
I'm almost finishing the DOOM 1 so I can finally move to DOOM 2.

[h2] DOOM [/h2]


I went into DOOM expecting a game that would feel incredibly dated and that wouldn’t hold up all too well in the modern day, but I was pleasantly surprised. The only parts of DOOM that made it feel old were its simple level design and lack of the ability to look up and down, which hurts aiming a bit.

[h2] DOOM II [/h2]


DOOM II has the addition of new enemies and the glorious Super Shotgun, but is otherwise very similar to DOOM. It shares the same artstyle and has most of the same weapons, but the new features let it differ from DOOM and feel like a new experience. It was a very enjoyable game, but there were a few levels that were confusing and I got very lost and had to watch a walkthrough in order to figure out where I needed to go and what I needed to do. 

[h2] Master Levels for DOOM II [/h2]


This is by far among my least favorite of the games included in this collection and is one of the few games I got little to no enjoyment from. Master Levels for DOOM II is just DOOM II but with some of the most frustrating level designs known to man. It was all total nonsense and I have no idea how anybody was supposed to navigate the game normally. One example that still stands out to me was having to walk along a thin, invisible pathway along a wall to access a small room that contained the red key. The only indicator was barely noticeable detail in the map. There were only a handful of levels where I didn’t need to look up a guide or watch a video. This game alone almost made me not recommend DOOM + DOOM II and I never want to return to it.

[h2] TNT: Evilution [/h2]


TNT: Evilution is when the level design started getting really good. The levels started having more and more small details that make it feel like an actual place instead of a basic environment with a general theme. The game was much more enjoyable and easier to navigate, unlike Master Levels for DOOM II. However, it started to drag and get boring as it went on. Aside from the level design, the game feels very similar to DOOM II and got repetitive, something that persisted through the following games.

[h2] The Plutonia Experiment [/h2]


God is not as forgiving as I once thought.

[h2] No Rest For The Living [/h2]


This one is a breath of fresh air after the previous games. No Rest For The Living still does feel like DOOM II, but is a lot more bearable because it has even more detailed levels than the previous games and doesn’t have absolutely absurd enemy spam like The Plutonia Experiment. It's also really short, which makes it a lot more digestible.

[h2] Sigil [/h2]


While No Rest For The Living is a breath of fresh air, Sigil is completely refreshing. Sigil only uses weapons and enemies from the original DOOM, which not only makes it feel different from the other games, it also makes it so you don’t have to worry about Chaingunners and Revenants. It also has some of my favorite level designs of all of the games included in this collection. I really enjoy having to shoot the hidden eyes in order to progress, and they’re never too difficult to find. It kind of gives it a sense of identity and stands out a little more compared to the other games.

[h2] Sigil II [/h2]


Sigil II is very similar to Sigil, but has a higher difficulty. It handles the difficulty increase better than previous games do. Instead of having insanely complex levels or an absurd number of enemies, it gives you more digestible encounters that are fair and more of a challenge and makes you mindful of your ammo, something you didn’t really have to think about in the other games. The only issue I have with Sigil II is that it gives you a lot of enemies that require a lot of ammo to defeat, or at least a significant amount, like Cacodemons and Barons of Hell.

[h2] Legacy of Rust [/h2]


Legacy of Rust brings back an issue I had with most of the other games. It is difficult, but in an annoying way that feels unfair rather than a reasonable and enjoyable way. It is rather upsetting considering how close this was to being likely the best in this collection. The new weapons and enemies made it feel new and not repetitive, it had unique levels which had new art, the music was phenomenal. Most levels were extremely enjoyable until you reached a certain part that was so annoying that it ruined the entire level. Soul Silo would have become my favorite DOOM level if it weren’t for the [spoiler] eight Cyberdemons [/spoiler] at the end of the level. I ran out of ammo after only killing half of them. I tried running around and getting them to shoot each other for 10 minutes but not one died. It was so close to being super enjoyable but just ended up being really frustrating.

It's a pretty good source port for Doom if you're just getting into the classic, and its PWAD manager is a nice touch, which might hopefully introduce new players to the massive world of custom Doom wads. It goes without saying that if you want a more optimal experience playing community Doom maps, you're better off going the extra mile and downloading a more "specialist" source port like DSDA-Doom, Woof, Retro Doom etc.
ts still holds up really well and is very fun, still feels like the defintive boomer shooter in my opinion ands defenetly one of the best games of all time
Well, its DOOM. There is nothing I can say that wouldn't just be the same regurgitated praise that's been sung for the past 30 years. Game rocks, nothing more to say.
I think the classic was okay and the puzzles weren't too hard, though I did have some parts where I struggled. Other than that it's a fun game and it has a cool vibe to it.

It's Doom. Its been Doom for 33 years and this plays the same but it has some active PvP matchmaking and updated Music, built in Mods and other features. Nothing is perfect, but this is still very much the grand daddy of FPS games.

Bought this game many times over the years on PC, SNES, Jaguar, and Playstation and I just can't help keep coming back to it again and again.
This has to be the definitive OG DOOM release, seems to have the lot and for less than £4 it was a total bargain.
They've tidied it up really nicely and it plays fast as hell, the updated music is absolutely incredible and drives you around the levels like you've got a turbo installed in your backside.

This is the undisputed heavyweight king of oldschool FPS shooters, it's what got everyone into the genre in the early 90's and is still loads of fun to play today. It's as challenging as you want it to be as there is a cheat menu that can be handy if you are new and learning the levels. If you're a seasoned slayer, you can crank it up to 11 and go hell for leather adding little bits and bobs like enemies respawning to keep you on your toes, one for the masochists !

The only thing I think this release could have done with is an option to add ray tracing to the visuals which I see you can do with a mod now. If the dev's could implement this into the options menu it would be the perfect game.

Legacy of Rust is great... up until about mission 6. Then you die to banshees around corners and convoluted, nonsense, hard to navigate design. It's wonderful, until it's absolute ♥♥♥♥, and the two sides come together with such a hard thud that I wonder how this happened. By mission 7 I was ready to uninstall. What a terrible waste.

1st with some negatives.
This remaster has no raw mouse input.
Even with Smoothing & Accel, Max Turn Acceleration, and Mouse Smoothing disabled the aim is still inconsistent, both under and over rotating by ~10% sometimes over 20%. (Enhance pointer precision is disabled on my computer).  This is the only game that continues to do it, the few other games that did do it were due to Raw Input being disabled by default or had mouse acceleration on by default and they no longer have this issue.  This may not be noticeable for those that rely heavily on strafe aiming, but those that mostly use rotation to aim (especially 45°+ turns) will notice it.
Other Nightdive remasters like Quake, Heretic+Hexen, and Blood Refreshed Supply do not have this problem.
I have raised this with Bethesda and am hoping this will be fixed.  In the meantime I will probably just use DSDA-Doom, Pr-Boom+, or Woof for single player.

There are still broken secrets in the game.  I know some were intentional as a joke from those who designed the maps, yet some just plain broken.

Now for the positives.
*Doom+Doom II has a lot of maps. (Including Legacy of Rust, which is a quality expansion)
*Cross-platform multiplayer
*Level modifiers (fast-monsters, pistol start, etc)

I really hope they  implement the option for Raw Mouse input.
Building muscle memory requires consistency.

Those that want to test\calibrate the aiming whilst removing user error you can temporarily map a button to do a 180° or 360° camera turn in Steam Input and then do Angle Calibration.

Backloggd:

DOOM: https://backloggd.com/u/TGA_backloggd/review/4657913/ 

So, I decided to try and clear through my backlog by playing through some classic FPS games, starting with the Wolfenstein 3D and then Spear of Destiny and now we're onto the classic of all FPS games, DOOM.

Stream: https://youtube.com/live/rk4PmQI8p24

The game that evolved what could be modern FPS with not only multiple weapons, but also ammo, gore and many secrets, along with even a timer, which gave birth to the speed running community that, though I am not a member of it, I am impressed with the different techniques people have developed over the years.

Unlike Wolfenstein 3D, the deaths never felt cheap to me and having multiple types of ammo meant that there was a good chance that I could have ammo and weapons ready when fighting demons, but they did seem to borrow that chapter system from Wolfenstein 3D which I really wasn't a fan of and here it doesn't feel good to be starting again from the pistol, however, the levels are actually balanced well enough that you aren't going to die immediately when that happens.

The game still holds up well today and I even learnt that the DOOM Slayer had a pet bunny! You learn something every day.

DOOM II: https://backloggd.com/u/TGA_backloggd/review/4657950/

Here we return to the world of DOOM, but the demons are now on Earth! You go down there, all badass and not only spend time saving mankind, but you also encounter new enemies and even have a few new weapons to transform the spawn of hell into stains on the walls!

Stream: https://youtube.com/live/Rj2WEE1XDDs

Much the same from the last game can be said here, this was a whole lot of fun and I immediately recognise that THIS was the version of DOOM I played when much younger, remembering where the chainsaw was in one of the first levels and so on and many of the early stages looking so familiar. It was great to come back and finally finish this off, though sadly because I didn't focus on streaming that month I only ended with this game and didn't get to try the others I wanted to focus on like the Quake series and so on, but another benefit of finishing this game was that now the stage is set to try out all the many DOOM WADS that are out there.

This is something that just adds to the real industry leader here, allowing people to modify and make their own games is something that's still done with the DOOM II engine. Especially through third-party groups like GZDOOM and ect. These games were real benchmarks in gaming history and should be remembered as such.
A true and personal classic of being first class FPS! Still holds-up!

Try the community content/WADs/mods, there's an infinite library of the stuff and it's never been easier (for lazy people) to play using the in-game browser. Brings new life into this 30+ year old game.

Amazing start to an Amazing anthology. DOOM is the type of game that you MUST play once in your lifetime. This makes it more fun when you pair it up with mods and overall, you have a game where you can explore depths of hell, while also having mods introduce different themes of hell.

Really dont know what kind of review people are expecting for this Game...It blew everyones mind back in the 90s and completely redefined the genre/created it. Its been around for over 30 years, if you havent heard of this by now then you've been living in some kind of bubble. People have dedicated there time to finding things that can run DOOM...like printers and fridges and calculators.

Its a great version of the game and plays extremely well. The arranged soundtrack is a nice treat for the ears rather than midi tracks though.

So grab your super shotgun, grunt on every wall like youre having your way with it to look for secrets and try not to get gang banged by the angry demons in these rooms.

For a game made in 1993 the gameplay has aged surprisingly well. Shooting is fun and satisfying, and finding secrets is enjoyable too. I had a blast with both Doom I and II but of course the game still has aged poorly in some areas. Many times I would kill all of the demons I saw and then end up wondering the map looking for a way to progress. As sometimes its hard to know where you need to go to move on. Overall I had a blast playing through both DOOM games and I am glad that the gameplay still holds up even in modern day.
I just finished the first and second doom and I'm ready to move on to doom 64 and then the rest. I'm going to play all the games in order of the lore. 10/10 game
I mean, this game did define the FPS genre. Nothing I really have to say here, it truly is the best game ever made. Highly recommend this game, 10/10.

It's DOOM + DOOM II. Complete collections of the original games with bonus content. No strings attached.

The DOOM + DOOM II bundle is exactly what the originals deserved. Running on the new KEX engine with native widescreen support, smooth performance, easy mod loading, and a pile of official expansions and add-ons included, this is hands down the best way to play these classics in 2025. The gunplay is still some of the tightest ever made, the level design holds up remarkably well, and the soundtrack absolutely rips. If you've got even a passing interest in the roots of the FPS genre, this is essential.

Also playing them on a Steamdeck feels so right.
Claaaassics! I'm a big baby and can't hack it in higher difficulties, but who doesn't love shooting demons with shotguns?
With how old DOOM is and how long it's been around, you know what it is. You know what you want it for. And it's what your going to get.

It's the quintessential "boomer shooter" and the pinnacle of the genre. I played Doom and Doom II, along with the release of Final Doom, for countless hours as a kid. Even after Duke Nukem and Quake, Doom still endured. This latest release/re-master of the games includes all new WADs and mission packs from the original creators, which only add to the legacy of Doom. Built-in mod support for more WADs and map packs provides almost endless hours of demon slaughtering fun.

One of the best decisions I have made is coming back to play classic Doom and watch a regular space marine slowly turn into a man so unhinged that when Hell wiped out all of Earth, his only reaction was to reload and keep it moving. No grief. Just bullets. The demons had the audacity to go after my rabbit too. which in the end didnt end well and ultimately led to me gaping every demon in hell anally.  And the osts? MAX that shi up. Your pubes will grow back. Ones you ofc didnt even have before. Your body will produce testosterone it didnt know it was capable of. Doctors will be amazed. You will not explain yourself. You are simply built different now because you pressed play on a 1993 video game and your DNA agreed. Peak aura. Peak levels. Peak everything. This game said "what if we made a man so hard that Hell became the victim" and never looked back.
